description: Uses quadratic residues in GF(p) to construct conference matrices and Hadamard matrices. For p ≡ 3 mod 4, the Jacobsthal matrix Q built from Legendre symbols is skew-symmetric with Q*Q^T = pI - J, yielding a skew Hadamard matrix of order p+1. This is the workhorse construction for prime-power orders.
mathematical_formalization: Q_{ij} = \chi((i-j) \bmod p), \quad H = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \mathbf{1}^T \\ -\mathbf{1} & Q + I \end{pmatrix}, \quad HH^T = (p+1)I
analogical_connections: Legendre symbol ↔ binary classification in ML (separating quadratic residues from non-residues)
Skew-symmetry of Q ↔ antisymmetric neural network layers
Conference matrix ↔ equiangular tight frames in signal processing
implementation_hypothesis: For p=167 (≡3 mod 4, prime): construct Jacobsthal matrix Q of order 167, then H(168) = [[1, 1^T],[-1, Q+I]]. Verified: H(168)*H(168)^T = 168*I. This gives the core building block for approaches to H(668)=H(4*167).
experiment_seed: Implement Paley I for p=167 in Python/NumPy. Verify orthogonality. Measure condition number. Test as component in larger constructions targeting 668.

description: A 4×4 block array that converts four ±1 matrices A,B,C,D satisfying AA^T+BB^T+CC^T+DD^T=4nI into a Hadamard matrix of order 4n. When A,B,C,D are circulant matrices of order n, the problem reduces to finding four ±1 sequences with zero-sum periodic autocorrelation at all nonzero lags. This is the primary construction method for orders 4p where p is prime.
mathematical_formalization: GS(A,B,C,D) = \begin{pmatrix} A & BR & CR & DR \\ -BR & A & D^TR & -C^TR \\ -CR & -D^TR & A & B^TR \\ -DR & C^TR & -B^TR & A \end{pmatrix}, \quad R = \text{anti-diagonal permutation}
analogical_connections: GS array structure ↔ quaternion algebra (i,j,k basis elements)
PAF flatness condition ↔ white noise spectrum in signal processing
Circulant structure ↔ convolutional layers in deep learning
implementation_hypothesis: For H(668): find four ±1 circulant matrices of order 167 with flat total power spectral density = 668 at every frequency. The search reduces to finding sequences whose DFT magnitudes squared sum to a constant. Current best heuristic search achieves energy ~486K (from target 0).
experiment_seed: Implement GS array in NumPy. Run simulated annealing with FFT-based incremental energy updates. Compare random init vs. Legendre-based init vs. cyclotomic init.

description: The Kronecker product of two Hadamard matrices of orders m and n yields a Hadamard matrix of order mn. This is the simplest recursive construction (Sylvester's method). For order 668 = 4×167, the obstruction is that 167 is prime and odd, so no H(167) exists. The nearest Kronecker-constructible order is 672 = 4×168.
mathematical_formalization: H_{mn} = H_m \otimes H_n, \quad (H_m \otimes H_n)(H_m \otimes H_n)^T = H_mH_m^T \otimes H_nH_n^T = mn \cdot I_{mn}
analogical_connections: Kronecker product ↔ multi-scale wavelet decomposition
Tensor structure ↔ product codes in coding theory
Factorization obstruction ↔ prime gaps in number theory
implementation_hypothesis: H(672) = H(4) ⊗ H(168) is a valid 672×672 Hadamard matrix. By truncating to the first 668 rows and columns, we obtain a ±1 matrix with H*H^T having diagonal entries 668 and off-diagonal entries bounded by ±4 (a 4-modular approximation).
experiment_seed: Construct H(672), verify, truncate to 668×668. Measure off-diagonal distribution. Compare with Eliahou's 64-modular construction quality.

description: Four symmetric circulant ±1 matrices A,B,C,D of order n satisfying A²+B²+C²+D²=4nI yield a Hadamard matrix of order 4n via the Williamson array. The symmetry constraint (palindromic first rows) halves the search space. Known for many n≤63 but NOT for n=167, which is exactly what's needed for H(668).
mathematical_formalization: A,B,C,D \text{ symmetric circulant}, \quad A^2+B^2+C^2+D^2 = 4 \cdot 167 \cdot I_{167}, \quad H = \text{Williamson}(A,B,C,D) \in \{\pm 1\}^{668 \times 668}
analogical_connections: Symmetric circulant constraint ↔ palindromic sequence design in genomics
Sum-of-squares condition ↔ Lagrange four-square theorem in number theory
Search space reduction via symmetry ↔ symmetry breaking in SAT solvers
implementation_hypothesis: Search for Williamson matrices of order 167 with palindromic first-row constraint. The search space is 2^84 (half of 167 positions due to symmetry). Use algebraic structure of Z_167 to prune. This is a sub-problem of the open H(668) problem.
experiment_seed: Enumerate palindromic ±1 sequences of length 167. Check circulant autocorrelation constraints. Use branch-and-bound or SAT reduction.

description: Golay complementary pairs/sets are sequences whose aperiodic or periodic autocorrelation functions sum to a delta function. For H(668), we need 4 sequences of length 167 whose periodic autocorrelations sum to zero at all nonzero lags. This connects to OFDM channel estimation, radar waveform design, and compressed sensing.
analogical_connections: Flat power spectrum ↔ white noise in physics
Complementary set condition ↔ perfect reconstruction filter banks
Golay pair existence restriction ↔ no-go theorems in quantum information
implementation_hypothesis: Decompose the flat PSD condition: need sum_i |S_i(k)|^2 = 668 for all k. The DC component requires (sum A)^2 + (sum B)^2 + (sum C)^2 + (sum D)^2 = 668. Valid decompositions: e.g., 1² + 9² + 15² + 19² = 668. Then optimize non-DC frequencies.
experiment_seed: Fix DC row-sums to a valid 4-square decomposition of 668. Run SA with swap moves preserving row sums. Measure PSD flatness convergence rate.

description: Partitioning the multiplicative group of GF(p) into cyclotomic classes provides structured initial sequences for Hadamard constructions. For p=167, the group Z*_167 has order 166 = 2×83. Since 4 does not divide 166, standard 4-class cyclotomic constructions fail, which is a root cause of the difficulty of H(668).
analogical_connections: Cyclotomic class structure ↔ frequency band allocation in wireless
Group factorization failure ↔ RSA hardness (related to group structure)
Partition obstruction ↔ impossibility results in distributed computing
implementation_hypothesis: Since 4∤166, use non-uniform partitions: split Z*_167 into 4 sets of sizes 42,42,41,41 by discrete log mod 4. These approximate cyclotomic classes and can seed optimization. Alternatively, use subgroup <g^2> of order 83 and its coset to partition into 2 halves, then subdivide further.
experiment_seed: Compute discrete logs mod 167 using primitive root g=5. Partition by index mod 4. Measure resulting sequence autocorrelation flatness. Compare with random partition.

description: The Hadamard condition for GS-array circulant sequences translates to constant total power spectral density in the frequency domain. This spectral flatness condition connects to information-theoretic capacity (Shannon), signal design for CDMA systems, and the uncertainty principle. The energy landscape has many local minima, explaining why local search fails for p=167.
analogical_connections: Flat PSD ↔ maximum entropy distribution in thermodynamics
PSD flatness ↔ channel capacity maximization (water-filling)
DFT domain optimization ↔ spectral methods in PDE solving
implementation_hypothesis: Reformulate the search as a continuous optimization: relax ±1 to [-1,1], minimize sum (P(k)-668)^2, then round. The rounding destroys flatness, creating a gap analogous to the integrality gap in combinatorial optimization.
experiment_seed: Implement continuous relaxation with gradient descent on PSD flatness. Measure gap between continuous optimum (P(k)=668 achievable) and integer (±1) feasibility.

description: Quantum approximate optimization (QAOA) and quantum annealing have been proposed for Hadamard matrix search. The H-SEARCH problem encodes the orthogonality constraint as a QUBO (quadratic unconstrained binary optimization) and maps it to a quantum Hamiltonian. For order M, this requires O(M) qubits on gate-based quantum computers.
mathematical_formalization: H_{\text{cost}} = \sum_{i<j} \left(\sum_k h_{ik} h_{jk}\right)^2, \quad h_{ik} \in \{-1,+1\} \mapsto Z_k \text{ Pauli operator}
analogical_connections: QUBO formulation ↔ Ising model in statistical mechanics
Quantum tunneling through barriers ↔ escaping local minima in SA
Qubit efficiency ↔ dimensionality reduction in classical computing
implementation_hypothesis: For H(668), the GS-array structure reduces the search from 668² binary variables to 4×167 = 668 binary variables. Encode as QUBO: minimize sum of squared off-diagonal entries of (GS)*(GS)^T. Run on quantum simulator with 668 qubits.
experiment_seed: Formulate QUBO for GS-array sequences of length 167. Test on quantum simulator (Qiskit/Cirq) at small scale (p=7,11). Extrapolate resource requirements for p=167.

description: An m-modular Hadamard matrix H satisfies H*H^T ≡ nI (mod m), relaxing exact orthogonality to modular congruence. Eliahou (2025) constructed a 64-modular Hadamard matrix of order 668, improving the previous record of m=32 from 2001. This relaxation connects to coding theory (codes over Z_m) and lattice-based cryptography.
analogical_connections: Modular relaxation ↔ approximate nearest-neighbor in ANN search
Increasing modulus toward exact solution ↔ lifting in algebraic geometry
m-modular matrices ↔ codes over rings Z/mZ in coding theory
implementation_hypothesis: Our truncated H(672)[:668,:668] achieves off-diagonal entries in {-4,-2,0,2,4}, making it 4-modular (equivalently 8-modular since all off-diag are even). This is weaker than Eliahou's 64-modular but trivially constructible. The hierarchy: 4-modular → 8 → 16 → 32 → 64 → exact.
experiment_seed: Construct the truncated matrix. Verify modular level. Compare with Eliahou's construction. Search for modifications that increase the modular level.

description: A conference matrix C of order n satisfies C*C^T = (n-1)I with zero diagonal and ±1 off-diagonal entries. For odd prime p ≡ 3 mod 4, the Jacobsthal matrix is a conference matrix of order p. Bordering C with ±1 vectors (Paley's technique) converts it to a Hadamard matrix. For H(668), the conference matrix of order 167 is available but the bordering to reach 668 requires a non-standard technique.
mathematical_formalization: C = Q_{167} \text{ (Jacobsthal)}, \quad CC^T = 166 \cdot I - J + 167I = 167I - J, \quad \text{border to get } H(168)
analogical_connections: Conference matrix ↔ strongly regular graph adjacency matrix
Bordering technique ↔ augmenting paths in network flow
Equiangular lines from conference matrices ↔ quantum state tomography
implementation_hypothesis: The Jacobsthal matrix Q of GF(167) is the starting point. Standard bordering gives H(168). For H(668) = H(4×167), we need to combine Q with a structured 4×4 design. The Goethals-Seidel array with A = Q+I and B,C,D derived from Q's structure is one path.
experiment_seed: Construct Q_167. Try A = Q+I, B = (shifted Q)+I variations. Measure AA^T+BB^T+CC^T+DD^T deviation from 668*I.

description: T-sequences are four (0,±1) sequences of length n with zero-sum non-periodic autocorrelation and disjoint support. They yield T-matrices, which combined with Williamson-type matrices via the Cooper-Wallis theorem produce Hadamard matrices of order 4nw. For H(668)=H(4×167×1), we need T-matrices of order 167 or a factorization 167=n×w with T-sequences of order n.
analogical_connections: Disjoint support ↔ orthogonal frequency division in OFDM
T-sequence as sparse code ↔ sparse representation in compressed sensing
Cooper-Wallis theorem ↔ product construction in coding theory
implementation_hypothesis: Since 167 is prime, the only factorization is 167=167×1. Need T-sequences of length 167. These are constructed from special properties of Z_167 (cf. Kharaghani's construction). The sequence design requires partitioning {0,...,166} into 4 disjoint support sets and assigning ±1 values.
experiment_seed: Implement T-sequence search for small primes (p=7,11,23). Verify Cooper-Wallis construction. Attempt to scale to p=167 using structural constraints from Z_167.

description: Supplementary difference sets (SDS) in an abelian group G generalize classical difference sets. A 4-{v; k1,k2,k3,k4; λ} SDS yields circulant matrices for the Goethals-Seidel array. For H(668), one needs 4-{167; k1,k2,k3,k4; λ} SDS in Z_167. The Spence construction alternatively needs 4-{166; 83,83,83,84; 166} SDS in Z_166. Neither is known.
mathematical_formalization: S_1,\ldots,S_4 \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_{167}, \quad \forall g \neq 0: \sum_{i=1}^{4} |\{(a,b) \in S_i \times S_i : a-b \equiv g\}| = \lambda
analogical_connections: Difference sets ↔ perfect hash families in computer science
SDS ↔ balanced incomplete block designs (BIBD)
Group structure of Z_167 ↔ error-correcting code structure
implementation_hypothesis: Search for 4-SDS in Z_167 using: (1) cyclotomic initial construction from QR/QNR, (2) local search via set element swaps, (3) algebraic constraints from the character theory of Z_167. The character sum conditions reduce to checking O(p) equations.
experiment_seed: Implement SDS verification. Start with QR/QNR-based initial sets. Run optimization over set membership. Track lambda-deviation across all group elements.
